title: Train vs. Validation/Test Preprocessing
concept: An image pipeline must handle training data and evaluation data differently. While training data receives random augmentations, validation and test data must only be resized and normalized with zero randomness.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is normalization applied as the final step in the pipeline?

options.text: Because color/brightness jitter algorithms expect positive pixel ranges (0-255 or 0-1) and break on normalized negative values
Because it makes the images smaller for faster disk writes
Because it automatically labels the dataset

exercises.explain: Yes! Normalizing last avoids breaking color transformation math.

cards.title: A Moving Target
cards.body: If you evaluate your model's progress on a validation set that randomly changes every time you look at it, you can't tell if an accuracy change is due to model progress or just lucky random cropping. Validation must remain a stable, consistent benchmark.

cards.title: Pipeline Separation
cards.body:
We split our pipeline into two branches:
1. Train Pipeline: Random Augmentation -> Resize -> Normalize.
2. Val/Test Pipeline: Resize -> Normalize (no randomness, no flips, no jitter).

cards.title: The standard exam
cards.body: Training is like studying in different environments — with music, in a quiet library, or with friends (augmentation adds variety). Validation is the official exam. During the exam, everyone gets the same desk, same paper, and same quiet room (zero randomness) so scores are fair and comparable.

cards.title: Medical Imaging
cards.body: When evaluating a lung X-ray model, you must not randomly crop the validation images (which could crop out the disease area) or jitter colors (which could obscure subtle tissue density differences). Test data must match clean, real-world inputs.

cards.title: Same resize rules, different tools
cards.body: Both pipelines resize images, but they do it differently. Training uses random crop + resize (selects random areas). Validation uses a deterministic center crop + resize (always crops the exact center). This ensures consistency while matching the scale of training images.

left.text:
Random Flips & Rotations
Random Resized Crop
Color & Hue Jitter
Normalize last

right.text:
NO flips or rotations
Deterministic Center Crop
NO color/hue jitter
Normalize last

exercises.prompt: Which transformation is allowed in the validation/test pipeline?
exercises.explain: Correct! Center cropping and normalization are deterministic, ensuring consistency.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You should use the exact same mean and standard deviation values for normalization in both train and validation pipelines.
exercises.explain: True. Normalization statistics must remain identical across splits to keep the data distributions aligned.

exercises.sentence: Validation preprocessing must be ___ to ensure evaluation metrics are stable and comparable.
exercises.explain: Validation preprocessing must be deterministic (constant and reproducible).

exercises.prompt: What is the danger of applying random horizontal flips to your validation set?

options.text: It introduces noise into your validation score, making it hard to tell if accuracy improvements are real
It permanently corrupts the files stored on your hard drive
It forces the network to only output negative prediction logits

exercises.explain: Correct! Randomness makes metrics noisy and inconsistent.

exercises.prompt: You are building a dog breed classifier. What is the correct setup for train and validation datasets?

options.text: Train gets random crops/flips/color jitter + normalization; Validation gets deterministic center crops + normalization using the exact same mean/std values
Both get identical random augmentations to make evaluation challenging
Train gets normalization; Validation gets no preprocessing at all
Train gets center crops; Validation gets random crops to test robustness

exercises.explain: Exactly! Train is augmented; validation is deterministic and matching. Next: a complete capstone recap of the preprocessing pipeline!
